## [03:00 - 06:00]

So during the next few minutes of this lesson, I'm going to show you what the first steps are to use an LLC once it's formed. Okay. So what do we do? Now we've gone just like my partner and I did. We went and we filed our LLC. We got our articles of organization schedule. We got that taken care of. That's been filed. It's all great. So what do we do next? Well, we went to mastermind law group and they gave us an operating agreement that was specific for our business and it was catered to what we wanted to have in there if it was by ourselves or with a partner. So we've got this operating agreement. What do we do with it? Well, the first thing you're going to do is you're going to print it off and you're going to sign that thing and then you're going to save it. You're going to put it in a binder. Okay. And you're going to keep it. And put it on your, your computer. So you've got that for you. Cause when people ask, you're going to be able to provide that because an operating agreement is a private document. You don't register that with anybody that stays behind the doors, behind the veil. Kind of like the wizard of Oz. Do not look behind this mirror. It's hidden until you need it. So an operating agreement's private, but you need to sign it. Need to make sure it's there because where I've had problems with this in the past is I'll have partners that fight and I'll say, okay, let me look at the operating agreement. And they're like, well, here's the unsigned version. I'm like, okay, where's the sign version? We never signed it. Well, if I'm the other partner and I don't like this operating agreement, I'm going to say, let's go to those default laws I talked about in the last lesson that the state set aside about limited liability companies. And we're going to rely on that because they're way more archaic and more advantageous in a dispute than having an agreement, a contract with your partner. So make sure number one, you've printed that off and you've signed it, especially if you have partners on this. You're still going to want to sign it because you're going to need that agreement if you're just by yourself to do the next step, which is go to a financial institution and open up a bank account. Now, why would you say that? Well, one of the ways that people can attack LLCs is to say that they're an alter ego of you. If the LLC is over here and you're over here, they're going to say, really, you're doing this, you're working, you're merging this business with your personal life. And so I do this all the time. So I would say about 30% of the business formations that I set up for LLCs are people that went out and did it with another service and it didn't figure it out. They went to a law firm and they didn't finish it up and then, or they try to do it themselves and didn't understand what they needed to do. And they come to me and they say, fix it. Well, that's kind of like sometimes putting toothpaste back into a tube. It's really difficult to do. And that's one of the things that we preach on. Do it right and understand what you're doing. So if you jump in here and you do this right the first time and you have an a bank account that's set up that's separate and distinct into a business that's separate and distinct from your personal bank account, they can't make this argument that they merge. But if you're like a lot of clients that come to me and they say, well, we set up the LLC, we had an operating agreement. Did you get a bank account for it? And you're like, no, we just add our personal bank account and say, okay, so

## [06:00 - 09:01]

this whole time you've been operating as a business, but you've been collecting money in your personal account. Well, that just opens that door for that argument that your business is just an alter ego of you. Same thing is true if you set up a business account and you pay for business assets and personal things out of your business account. You don't want to do that either. It opens that liability. So step one, every business I set up, big or small, I go and open a bank or credit union account, a checking account for that new business I set up. I put the minimum balance in there and I set aside for that. Whether I'm actively promoting that business at a time or I've been successful with that business at a time, I just do it from day one. Now, this is a question I get all the time. Like when should I set up the LLC? Should I wait until I'm successful? No. Every single business venture that I've ever done, I set up and I set up before I started doing business. So that illustration, I talked about my law partner and I were two dumb kids, man. Yeah, we graduated from good law schools and I clerked for the Nevada Supreme Court, but we hadn't practical experience about operating as lawyers before we hadn't done it before. Did we wait to set up the business before we were profitable and successful? No, we did it then. We started that. We had the foundation. Now, why would I do that? Because if you don't set up a business, there's two reasons if you wait to do a business, why that can be a problem. Number one, all of the activities you do before you set up the LLC, you're liable for those. So you enter into agreements or contracts with somebody else and some breach of contract comes up and you're liable. Anything before the business, you're liable. We didn't want that set of the business before we took on our first customer. We didn't know if we were going to be successful. We didn't know if we were going to be that, you know, five or 10% of businesses that actually succeed or not. We didn't know we were dumb. You know, students that just got out of law school, we were just starting. We took a gamble. It was a good gamble that paid off and it's something that I tell all clients to do. So the number one reason was that pre-incorporation liabilities, what they call it, we don't want that. Number two is we paid a ton of expenses for things, right? And we weren't in the business of being attorneys yet. So would the IRS then say, oh, you're trying to take a deduction or write an expense off that you're not able to take? Well, we didn't want to run that risk because the IRS will look at the date of incorporation and the timeframe in which you made the expense, the expenditure, and you paid for things on whether you can write that off. And the longer it is from the time you make the expense, from the time you set up the business, the more chance that that looks at, oh, that doesn't look fun. That looks funny. Maybe I want to audit that. And we'll talk about audit in one of the other lessons and why we don't want that either. So I set up businesses from the beginning. I make sure that we've got those separate distinct and we do it. Now we've got the business bank account. Now, for those of you that we're going to talk about setting up an LLC in a state where either you don't reside because you're out of the country or you like the laws of other states, we might have to be bank selective.

## [09:01 - 12:01]

What I mean by that is certain banks have certain rules about whether you have to have the company registered in the state in which the business is set up or where the bank is established. So if you set up a Wyoming LLC, for example, and you want to get a bank account for that, but you're in England or you're in Massachusetts, but you want the Wyoming, we may have to go and look at some banks that will allow for that and there are plenty to do it. So one of those things that we, we know the tricks of the trade and how it works out and why is behind it and we'll help you do that as well. But having that bank account is easy. You know, the biggest hurdle on setting up an LLC and using it that I find is coming up with a name for the LLC. It's not hard. Some of my LLCs are the towns in Scotland where my family's from, or, you know, this childhood street names that we, we were so familiar with as kids or animals or whatever. I have fun. You only set up so many businesses. It's pretty good. But when you've set up that business name, it's important that you stop advertising under Joe Blow and you do it under that business name. Why? Cause again, we're trying to keep a separation from ourselves. Now a lot of people like to use their names as their businesses. I generally don't recommend that. We can talk about it, but I generally come up with business names that are fun, you know, Blue Skies International or whatever it is, just have fun with it because it's fun coming up with business names. But when you have that business name, don't operate under your own name because that's a sole practitioner. Be an LLC, operate in the LLC. Now, why is that important? Because when you operate as an LLC, you're going to enter into agreements with third parties, whether it's for advertising or it's to get talent, to promote your business because you're like, Hey, I really need somebody that understands this element of this business. Let's go back to our illustration before. I have a course that I'm teaching about health related things, about how to eat nutritionally, how to exercise to reduce my weight so that I can have lower cholesterol, lower blood pressure, how I can lower my blood sugar level, all the things that, you know, should be natural consequences of doing this. I found this company over here that's got this hydration supplement that I really want to add into it. So I go out and I enter a contract with them to provide that product so I can part it, put a product into that service and that knowledge training that I'm giving. Well, I go and I enter that agreement. I don't want to enter that agreement as me, Joe Blow. I want to enter into that agreement as Joe Blow manager of Blue Sky International or corporation or LLC. So every time I enter into an agreement as an LLC, I'm not doing that as an individual. I'm doing that as the manager. Now, why is that? Again, I talked about this in the last lesson, but in this lesson understand that managers generally don't have liability for the LLC. They're protected under that operating agreement. Now, what about using the LLC next that we need to talk about? We really haven't addressed yet.

## [12:01 - 13:19]

Tax considerations and consequences. How do I use this business to protect against my liabilities? There are two great creditors that are out there. One is like we use the illustration, we enter into a contract and we breach that contract with somebody and they sue us. That's a private organization suing us, a private company suing us. But there are also public entities like the Internal Revenue Service that will come after you. So we need to talk about tax considerations. So my next lesson talks about tax considerations and consequences of running a business because I find that most of the time when other law firms or other entities set up businesses, they never talk about what the tax ramifications are. And there are some simple mechanisms we can put into place that can save thousands of dollars if done correctly. Now, the savings comes when you make money. So understand the whole purpose of this is when you get into business, isn't to lose money or go out of business, it's try to put yourself in a position to make money. Is that a guarantee? No, a lot of businesses fail. But going through this training is to put you in a position you can be on a better level to be able to succeed where others don't have financial principles. They don't understand how LLCs work. They don't know how to use an LLC. They don't understand what tax considerations are. So the next lesson we're going to talk about tax considerations.
